data.title: RSPN: Industrials Dashboard For January
data.description:
This article offers a top-down analysis of the industrial sector based on value, quality, and momentum metrics. It may also help analyze sector ETFs like Invesco S&P 500 Equal Weight Industrials ETF (RSPN) and Industrial Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLI), whose largest holdings are used to calculate these metrics.

Shortcut

The next two paragraphs in italics describe the dashboard methodology. They are necessary for new readers to understand the metrics. If you are used to this series or if you are short of time, you can skip them and go to the charts.

Base Metrics

I calculate the median value of five fundamental ratios for each subsector: Earnings Yield ("EY"), Sales Yield ("SY"), Free Cash Flow Yield ("FY"), Return on Equity ("ROE"), Gross MaRSPNn ("GM"). The reference universe includes large companies in the U.S. stock market. The five base metrics are calculated on trailing 12 months. For all of them, higher is better. EY, SY and FY are medians of the inverse of Price/Earnings, Price/Sales and Price/Free Cash Flow. They are better for statistical studies than price-to-something ratios, which are unusable or non-available when the "something" is close to zero or negative (for example, companies with negative earnings). I also look at two momentum metrics for each group: the median monthly return (RetM) and the median annual return (RetY).

I prefer medians to averages because a median splits a set into a good half and a bad half. A capital-weighted average is skewed by extreme values and the largest companies. My metrics are designed for stock-picking rather than index investing.

Value and Quality Scores

I calculate historical baselines for all metrics. They are noted respectively as EYh, SYh, FYh, ROEh, GMh, and they are calculated as the averages on a look-back period of 11 years. For example, the value of EYh for transportation in the table below is the 11-year average of the median earnings yield in transportation companies. The Value Score ("VS") is defined as the average difference in % between the three valuation ratios (EY, SY, FY) and their baselines (EYh, SYh, FYh). The same way, the Quality Score ("QS") is the average difference between the two quality ratios (ROE, GM) and their baselines (ROEh, GMh).

The scores are in percentage points. VS may be interpreted as the percentage of undervaluation or overvaluation relative to the baseline (positive is good, negative is bad). This interpretation must be taken with caution: the baseline is an arbitrary reference, not a supposed fair value. The formula assumes that the three valuation metrics are of equal importance.

Current data

The next table shows the metrics and scores as of last week's closing. Columns stand for all the data defined above.

Value and Quality chart

The next chart plots the Value and Quality Scores by subsector (higher is better).

Evolution since last month

The most notable change is a deterioration in value score for the aerospace & defense subsector.

Momentum

The next chart plots momentum based on median returns by subsector.

Interpretation

Based on my latest S&P 500 dashboard, industrials as a whole are one of the most overvalued sectors, along with materials and technology. However, the transportation industry is close to its 11-year averages in valuation and quality. Other subsectors are overvalued by 26% to 40%, aerospace & defense being the most overpriced. Overvaluation may be partly offset by a good quality score for building/equipment.

Focus on RSPN

Invesco S&P 500 Equal Weight Industrials ETF (RSPN) was launched on 11/01/2006 and tracks the S&P 500 Equal Weight Industrials Index. RSPN has a portfolio of 80 stocks and an expense ratio of 0.40%, while its capital-weighted counterpart XLI charges only 0.08% with the same holdings.

Positions are reset in equal weight at every quarterly rebalance. They are now between 1.06% and 1.52%, with 14% in the top 10 holdings, listed below. These are the constituents with the highest price return since the most recent rebalance. Company-specific risk is significantly lower than for XLI, whose top 10 positions represent 39% of assets, the heaviest one weighting 6.7%.

In summary, RSPN offers lower company risk, slightly better value and similar long-term returns and volatility compared to capital-weighted funds in the sector. It is well-suited for long-term investors seeking a balanced portfolio in industrials, but traders will prefer XLI’s higher trading volumes.

Dashboard List

I use the first table to calculate value and quality scores. It may also be used in a stock-picking process to check how companies stand among their peers. For example, the EY column tells us that a transportation company with an earnings yield above 0.0456 (or price/earnings below 21.93) is in the better half of the subsector regarding this metric. A Dashboard List is sent every month to Quantitative Risk & Value subscribers, with the most profitable companies standing in the better half among their peers regarding the three valuation metrics at the same time. The stocks below are part of the list sent to subscribers a few weeks ago.

data.title: Uber: Buy The Dip As Delivery Services Accelerate
data.description:
In general, I recommend caution in investing in the stock market in 2026. Valuation multiples look incredibly stretched, especially among large-cap tech stocks. That said, if we deploy careful stock selection, there are still great deals to be had, specifically among potential rebound plays.

Uber Technologies, Inc. (UBER), to me, is looking especially promising after a recent ~10% dip from near-term highs just shy of $100. Investors remain nervous on the company’s AV rollout, while also expressing caution around a slower macroeconomy.

In reality, Uber’s results don’t show any meaningful slowdown at all despite macro headwinds. I last wrote a "Buy" opinion on Uber in October, when the stock was trading closer to $94. Today, I’m happy to add to my position at lower prices, especially given the recent acceleration in delivery results. I reiterate my "Buy" rating here.

A couple of catalysts are worth spotlighting to emphasize the bull case for Uber. In particular, I’ve long since moved past thinking of Uber as merely a ride-hailing app. It’s true market opportunity is the overall convenience economy - whisking people and objects around utilizing its massive transportation network.

The biggest illustration of this is in grocery delivery. Recently, Uber has achieved meaningful acceleration in its deliveries segment, which is largely driven by the company’s heightened focus on grocery. Uber began with takeout orders, but the market for delivery is actually substantially larger. As shown below, the company believes the annual grocery delivery market opportunity is $10 trillion. That’s 5x the size of the $2 trillion restaurant delivery market. And though Uber is already the leading global delivery company, its quarterly delivery gross bookings of ~$23 billion implies it's still only ~1% penetrated into this opportunity.

We should also mention the company’s freight business. Now, of course, there are severe macro headwinds here at hand. Not only is the slowing consumer economy driving weakness in logistics, but tariffs are also reducing cross-border shipments into the U.S. Still, when the economy stabilizes, Uber has already built up an expansive freight business that is just on the cusp of breakeven profitability.

Despite its ~20% top-line growth and its upcoming catalysts in rolling out AVs (which should, at scale, reduce cost per mile driven for every booking), Uber still trades at a reasonable valuation. As shown below, after its recent crash, the stock is valued at ~16x adjusted EBITDA. To me, that’s more than a reasonable price to pay for the company’s current pace of >30% adjusted EBITDA growth.

Uber does indeed trade at a slightly richer multiple than Lyft (LYFT). Lyft is presenting a greater risk on the mobility side, as its rideshare bookings growth has closed the gap to Uber’s. Still, Uber’s overall market opportunity is far larger than Lyft's due to its wider portfolio of delivery services plus an enlarged global presence. Uber still sits at a much cheaper multiple than DoorDash (DASH), which I find reassuring.

Use the recent dip in Uber as a buying opportunity.

Q3 Download

Let’s now go through Uber’s latest quarterly results in greater detail. The bookings trend in the company’s mobility segment are shown below.

Segment gross bookings grew 19% on a constant currency basis, up to $25.11 billion. We note here there is basically no evidence of any macro-driven slowdown: gross bookings accelerated a point versus 18% growth in Q2.

We think it’s prudent to note here that Uber’s competition in the U.S. is heating up. Lyft’s gross bookings growth of 19% in Q3 finally caught up to Uber’s growth rate. We note as well that Lyft’s recent acquisition of Freenow also expands its competitive presence into key European markets. That said, we don’t necessarily see evidence that the market size is limited here; clearly rideshare is still large enough for Uber to still accelerate its bookings profile. Also of note is the fact that Lyft is also selectively targeting certain niche markets (such as college campuses away from urban metro areas) where competition isn’t as dense. There’s enough room here for a “Coke and Pepsi” situation of two major rideshare players in the U.S.

In the delivery segment, Uber shone even brighter. As shown below, delivery gross bookings accelerated to 24% growth, up four points sequentially from 4% growth in Q2.

Again, here, a competitive assessment is apt. Main rival DoorDash saw two points of sequential acceleration to 25% growth (from 23% in Q2), slightly faster than Uber (but also accelerating slightly less). We note DoorDash and Lyft have a partnership between Dash Pass and Lyft Pink, giving both rideshare and delivery benefits to compete against Uber One’s all-in-one appeal.

Also of note: Amazon (AMZN) is expanding the same-day delivery coverage of its Amazon Fresh network. Amazon Fresh and Whole Foods both remain massive grocery players that are off-limits to rideshare companies, remaining within Amazon Prime’s walled garden.

Overall profitability for Uber rose 33% y/y to $2.26 billion in adjusted EBITDA, representing a 4.5% margin against bookings that improved 40bps y/y.

The company’s improving margin is a strong signal that despite competition in all segments, the company isn’t leaning in too heavily on incentives and promotions to maintain market share.

Q4 Look-Ahead And Key Takeaways

Uber is next set to report Q4 earnings at some point in early February. To me, these are the core items to watch:

  • How does Uber’s bookings growth compare to peers? Mobility and delivery both grew in line with key peers in Q3. Moving faster than peers would be a very strong indicator that will provide upside for the stock.
  • Margin trends. Amid a tighter macro, competitors may be offering more flash deals and promotions, forcing Uber to compete.
  • AV development. We’re also on the lookout for any anecdotal remarks on AV rollout.

All in all, I think Uber's recent trends and its trajectory more than justify a ~16x adjusted EBITDA multiple that can re-rate higher. Stay long here and "Buy" the dip.

data.title: China's 2025 Rebound: Why Selectivity Has Mattered
data.description:
By Hyun Kang

After years of uncertainty and underperformance, Chinese equities delivered a strong rebound in 2025. Improvements in investor sentiment - supported in part by advances in AI and semiconductor technology - helped rebuild confidence, while the MSCI China Index reached decade-high levels late in the year.

MSCI China Index and MSCI ACWI Index Year-to-Date Total Local Returns

Source: WisdomTree, MSCI, FactSet. Returns from 12/31/2024-12/31/2025. Past performance is not indicative of future results. You cannot invest directly in an index.

Policymakers implemented a series of targeted monetary, fiscal, and capital-market measures aimed at stabilizing growth and restoring confidence. Unlike past cycles, these actions were incremental and focused on risk containment rather than broad-based reflation.

Even so, China's equity gains in 2025 were not merely a policy-driven rally. Valuations began the year at deeply discounted levels, and as investor confidence improved, equities were repriced higher, narrowing the gap versus global markets.

Beyond policy support, investor sentiment has also been lifted by the resilience of China's technology sector and a faster-than-expected easing of U.S.-China tariff frictions. Plans for multiple high-level meetings between the two countries' leaders in 2026—potentially as many as four—have added to a sense of improving dialogue. A modest appreciation of the renminbi may also provide a tailwind for Chinese equities.

Even after this repricing, Chinese equities continue to trade at meaningful discounts relative to global equities, as measured by the MSCI ACWI Index. While valuation remains supportive, the renewed interest in China has been driven less by absolute cheapness and more by confidence in the resilience of its technology sector and supply-chain positioning.

Historical Price to Earnings

Source: WisdomTree, MSCI, FactSet. Data from 11/30/2015-12/31/2025. Past performance is not indicative of future results. You cannot invest directly in an index.

At the company level, Chinese equities continue to trade at marked valuation discounts, most notably relative to U.S. equities. The largest constituents of the MSCI China Index trade at substantially lower forward multiples than those of the S&P 500, despite stronger forward earnings growth expectations.

Forward Valuation and Earnings Growth: S&P 500 vs. MSCI China Top Constituents

Source: WisdomTree, S&P, MSCI, FactSet. Data as of 12/31/2025. Past performance is not indicative of future results. You cannot invest directly in an index.

Importantly, China's rebound has not been uniform across ownership structures. Government ownership can meaningfully influence corporate priorities, often creating trade-offs between policy objectives and shareholder outcomes.

WisdomTree's ex-State-Owned Enterprises approach focuses on companies with limited government ownership, where firms tend to operate with greater autonomy and stronger alignment with market incentives. By excluding state-owned enterprises (SOEs), this framework seeks to reduce governance and capital-allocation risks.

The WisdomTree China ex-State-Owned Enterprises Index, tracked by the WisdomTree China ex-State-Owned Enterprises Fund (CXSE), has had a very strong year in 2025, outperforming its benchmark, the MSCI China Index, by 574 basis points year-to-date. Most of this outperformance can be attributed to its overweight to non-SOEs (ex-SOEs) and underweight to SOEs, with 328 basis points of attribution driven by allocation effects.

Year-to-Date Index Performance Attribution

Source: WisdomTree, MSCI, FactSet. Returns from 12/31/2024-12/31/2025. Past performance is not indicative of future results. You cannot invest directly in an index.

CXSE's outperformance has not been limited to China benchmarks alone. In 2025, the strategy also outperformed developed and emerging market peers, underscoring that its results were not simply a byproduct of China's rebound, but of how exposure was constructed.

Year-to-Date Cumulative Performance

Source: WisdomTree, S&P, MSCI, FactSet. Returns from 12/31/2024-12/31/2025. You cannot invest directly in an index. Past performance is not indicative of future results. Investment return and principal value of an investment will fluctuate so that an investor's shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data quoted.

China's experience in 2025 underscores that how investors gain exposure matters as much as whether they do. As valuations reset and confidence improved, performance dispersion reflected ownership structure and governance differences. CXSE's construction—tilting away from state ownership and toward market-oriented companies—has been well aligned with those dynamics.

Conclusion

Looking ahead, the U.S.–China relationship does not appear to be deteriorating, and China's technology sector has remained resilient. China's equity market is likely to continue to be defined by uneven macroeconomic recovery and meaningful dispersion. Investing in Chinese equities therefore requires a long-term view on China's technology sector, which is largely non-state owned. In that environment, strategies that emphasize governance, capital discipline, and market incentives may be better positioned to navigate ongoing uncertainty. CXSE offers one such approach for investors seeking a more selective way to access China's equity opportunity.

Important Risks Related to this Article

There are risks associated with investing, including possible loss of principal. Foreign investing involves special risks, such as risk of loss from currency fluctuation or political or economic uncertainty. The Fund focuses its investments in China, including A-shares, which include the risk of the Stock Connect program, thereby increasing the impact of events and developments associated with the region which can adversely affect performance. Investments in emerging or offshore markets are generally less liquid and less efficient than investments in developed markets and are subject to additional risks, such as risks of adverse governmental regulation and intervention or political developments. The Fund’s exposure to certain sectors may increase its vulnerability to any single economic or regulatory development related to such sector. As this Fund can have a high concentration in some issuers, the Fund can be adversely impacted by changes affecting those issuers. Please read the Fund’s prospectus for specific details regarding the Fund’s risk profile.

Hyun Kang, Research Analyst

Hyun Kang joined WisdomTree in July 2022 as a Research Analyst. As a part of the Index team, he assists with the creation and maintenance of the firm’s indexes and supports the group’s research initiatives across various strategies. Hyun graduated from Carnegie Mellon University, with a B.S. in Business Administration and an additional major in Statistics and Machine Learning.

Original Post

data.title: Allient Looks Like It's Run Far Enough
data.description:
Over time, Allient Inc. (ALNT) has been an excellent stock. Since Dick Warzala was named chief executive officer in May 2009, ALNT has returned over 3,700%. Obviously, the start of the period coincided with severe lows in the market and the ALNT stock price, but Allient has nearly doubled the returns of the Invesco QQQ ETF (QQQ) over that period:

To be sure, there have been drawdowns along the way: it was only 15 months ago that shares were at a four-year low. But Warzala has replicated the strategy of his former employer, Danaher (DHR), in using a combination of acquisitions, organic growth, and efficient operations to drive shareholder value (albeit without the spin-offs).

So looking backwards from a long-term perspective, ALNT looks like a winner. On the same basis looking forward, there too is reason for optimism, with the company potentially benefiting from a number of secular trends. Add in an apparent inflection in financial results over the last few quarters, and the big jump off spring lows seems to make sense.

The question, however, is whether the run in ALNT can continue. From here, it's likely gone far enough. Again, there is good news, but valuation is near historic highs while performance might not be quite as impressive as headline numbers suggest. Allient remains, as it has been, a business worth owning, but it's still a business where valuation matters — and personally I'd like to see that valuation come down.

The Bull Case For ALNT Stock

Since the day before Allient's first quarter release in early May, ALNT stock has gained 177%. No doubt broader optimism toward secular trends is a key factor, but there's also likely a sense that Allient has turned the corner.

After a difficult 2024, in which customers pulled back after playing catch-up amid supply chain normalization the year before, 2025 results have steadily improved. Revenue declined 9% year-over-year in Q1, then rose 3% in Q2 and jumped 11% in the third quarter. Profit margins have expanded nicely, with year-to-date Adjusted EBITDA up nearly 20% on a less than 1% increase in sales. Mix shifts and further efficiency improvements under a program called "Simplify to Accelerate Now" are driving the bottom-line growth, leading to record gross margins in the process:

So the bull case with ALNT at all-time highs is that there is a strong combination of a cyclical upswing along with multi-year benefits from secular trends. Allient's industrial products (almost half of year-to-date revenue) should benefit from data center demand; its aerospace and defense business is serving end markets with years of growth ahead (including drones); robotics growth is a tailwind.

And so if you avoid the "well, the stock already has almost tripled, so the easy money has been made" trap, the bull case for ALNT stock seems strong. Notably, it seems almost obvious. The short-term case is strong, with results steadily improving. The long-term catalysts are myriad and have a company-specific benefit: Warzala has noted (for instance, on the Q2 call) that the growth markets are largely in areas where Allient gets above-average margin. Management is solid; again, Warzala's track record is impressive, and the profit growth in 2025 has been driven in part by efficiency efforts as well as an intentional shift away from weaker, lower-margin markets like powersports.

To be sure, even bulls would have to admit that valuation is more aggressive than it historically has been:

But that should hardly break the bull case here. Allient's outlook almost certainly is better than it historically has been. And it's not as the current ~16x trailing twelve-month EBITDA multiple, or the ~25-27x price to normalized free cash flow multiple it suggests, are particularly outrageous. This is a business that may well be set to grow the top line double digits, there are some modest deleveraging benefits on the way as the company pays down debt, and management is solid. Ignoring the big gains since May, ALNT seems like an easy buy.

The Growth Question

I'm certainly sympathetic to that case, and I've written a few times of late about the importance in this market of sticking with winners, rather than assuming steep rallies will reverse. But in this specific case, I see two core, related, concerns.

The first is over the idea that growth necessarily has inflected. Year-over-year comparisons look strong, certainly, but 2024 was a very difficult year for Allient. Revenue declined 8% for the full year, and that was with help from acquisitions: pro forma figures from the 10-K suggest a fall of over 14%. Adjusted EBITDA declined 19% as reported, and likely in the range of 25% on an organic basis.

To be sure, outside factors contributed. Warzala has said that somewhere around $40 million in higher revenue in 2023 was driven by a "surge" in automation demand following supply-chain normalization that wasn't repeatable; that was about a seven-point headwind to growth the following year. It's not as if the business was headed steadily in the wrong direction before the last two quarters.

But the easy comparison obviously does affect this year's reported growth. When compared to Q3 2024, the most recent reported quarter looks like a step up in performance; against the figure two years earlier, however, it suggests something much less impressive: revenue is actually down over 4 points as reported. And yes, 2023, did include that boost in revenue in automation — but Allient has also added sales via acquisitions since. Versus the pro forma figure two years earlier, Q3 2025 sales were down $18 million, or more than 11%. The same sense holds taking the full-year view: 2025 consensus estimates still suggest a 7% decline from the 2023 pro forma figure, and only about 55% growth from the 2019 print.

Given that a decent chunk of that ~$200 million increase since 2019 comes from acquisitions (no doubt more than half, based on pro forma figures in 10-Ks since), this business overall hasn't been growing at an impressive rate. Similarly, there's a worry that the impressive margin expansion of this year may come to an end: Allient is already ~70 basis points above the 2019 print, and in the mid-13% range (with ~33% gross margins) doesn't have an obvious path to accelerated improvement on that front.

This is not to say that Allient is a bad business, and indeed bulls might argue this analysis helps the bull case. There has been some apparent cyclicality in recent results (in markets like vehicles and oil and gas, along with volatility in industrial demand more broadly) which might suggest there's even more room for the top line to improve as the external environment does. And if Allient really is a winner across multiple high-growth end markets, then the performance from 2023-2025 isn't dispositive anyway.

But, from here, the idea that this business has inflected is dispelled by a closer look at the performance over the last few years. Underlying growth simply hasn't been that impressive, and that colors the story somewhat with ALNT stock at the highs.

Can ALNT Get Cheaper?

And in that context, personally I'm a bit skeptical about paying historically high multiples to EBITDA and cash flow. The market is pricing in relatively consistent high-single-digit or even double-digit bottom-line growth here (depending on how exactly you run the model), and that would be a relatively large jump off the long-term trend.

Again, it's possible Allient makes that jump. Indeed, skepticism here likely turns out to be mostly right or very badly wrong. The confluence of "mega-trends" here, ranging from data centers to automation to defense, all suggest that there is a reasonable path for growth to actually take off. And on paper, there's a case for a double at least in that scenario (revenue to ~$900 million over four years, or 10-12% annualized growth; EBITDA margins to 16% from the current 13%-plus; 15x exit multiple).

Yet, so far, that jump hasn't really happened. This has been a good business for a long time — but so far this decade, Allient has largely remained a good business. The problem for ALNT stock after this big rally is that the market now is pricing more than that — and, from this perspective, too much.

data.title: CAVA: 3 Reasons The Stock Is A Strong Buy This January
data.description:
A few weeks ago, I published an article titled "My Market Outlook For 2026: AI Takes A Breather While The Consumer Comes Roaring Back".

In that article, I made the case that the AI trade would likely have a consolidation year as fundamentals catch up to the valuations, and that consumer sentiment was so negatively extended to the downside that a rebound looked likely in 2026.

In that article, I also highlighted restaurants as a core industry that’s been getting killed over the last few quarters.

As consumer spending has tightened, restaurants have been negatively impacted and many fast casual chains like Sweetgreen (SG) have seen negative same store sales growth. As a result, almost all restaurant stocks are down considerably over the last six months.

At the same time, I’ve also talked in recent articles about my love for CAVA Group (CAVA) - the fast growing fast casual Mediterranean restaurant chain that mirrors Chipotle’s (CMG) growth story.

Through multiple market cycles, the company has shown tremendous operating competency and sales growth has actually stayed positive through the turbulence, which makes it a rare find.

While the stock is down considerably from highs - largely on the back of the aforementioned consumer weakness - I believe that shares are now priced at an unbelievably attractive level. As the consumer rebounds through the next 12 to 24 months, I expect shares to reinflate as the company boasts strong leverage to a recovering consumer, strong operational discipline and excellent unit economics.

Today, I’ll refresh my thesis on CAVA, touch on the valuation, and explain the three key reasons I believe shares of the stock are now a Strong Buy.

Sound good? Let’s dive in.

Reason 1: Same Store Sales Leverage

In my most recent article on CAVA, I covered the scenario that I see most likely happening - that CAVA grows to 2,000 stores over the next decade. With under 400 currently, it’s an ambitious growth trajectory, but I believe that the company's product market fit and business model have the economics to support such a rapid expansion.

Obviously, in recent quarters - particularly in the last two quarters - the company's results have been somewhat lackluster. In the company’s Q3 earnings report, same store sales grew only 1.9% - a decrease from 18.1% one year earlier:

As you may expect, a 90% drop in the SSS growth is a concern for growth oriented investors, which is why shares are down so much as of late.

However, most of this same store sales result can be explained by the weak macro picture and a weakening consumer cohort that can find less and less reason to spend money on dining out as opposed to groceries.

As consumer sentiment and other macro numbers flatlined, so too have the same sales growth numbers:

Aside from this, poor employment numbers, weak business sentiment, and an unhealthy consumer are difficulties that many are currently experiencing in this K-shaped economy. The unemployment rate continues to tick up, and the purchasing manager's index remains below 50:

That said, I believe that the company’s most recent numbers show two things. First, the business has done better than expected in an extremely challenging environment.

Competitors like Sweetgreen have reported negative same store sales growth in recent periods, which shows that despite headwinds, CAVA's continued growth shows the durability of consumer preferences for the company's core Mediterranean fast casual offering (emphasis added):

For [Sweetgreen's] third quarter, we reported sales of $172.4 million and a same-store sales decline of 9.5%. Restaurant level margin was 13.1% and adjusted EBITDA was a loss of $4.4 million.

Secondly, and more importantly, previous numbers from - 2024 when the consumer was stronger - suggest just how much upside CAVA has towards consumer spending trends. If they rebound, I expect CAVA will handily produce double digit same store sales growth in quarters both via pricing and volume.

I expect a rebound in the consumer - frankly - as I just don’t believe things can get that much worse, aside from a full blown recession or depression. Consumer sentiment numbers recently tagged all-time lows since they started recording the figure in the 1950s, and hiring has been slow, especially for the Gen X cohort:

Considering that much of CAVA's recent slowdown management has attributed to this group, I expect a rebound in consumer sentiment, hiring, and BBB tax cuts to put more money in the pockets of America’s working youth:

I believe that recent readings represent a relative nadir, and to some degree, these sentiment reports have only one direction to go - back up.

Not only that, but new graduates from Gen Z and those recently fired have been finding it difficult to get hired in the current job market, which has been frozen as a result of AI.

As businesses take time to understand the impact that AI could have on productivity, it has delayed workforce planning, which has - in turn - delayed hiring. This has put significant pressure on the 25- to 35-year-old consumer cohort, which has shrunk discretionary spending significantly in recent quarters.

That said, in 2026, I expect that many businesses will begin to start hiring again as they get a better handle on just how many people they will need in this new environment...

Finally, on a purely monetary basis, recent tax cuts under the Big Beautiful Bill have shifted some household financial stress into a national liability... in the short term they will drive higher nominal income for workers up and down the spectrum.

This, in turn, should power CAVA's next leg higher. The company has considerable leverage to consumers doing well and if sentiment improves, as I believe they will, that has a chance to materially impact CAVA's stock price.

Reason 2: Margin Resilience

So - same store sales growth has remained positive, although increases have decelerated. One area in which I’m incredibly happy with CAVA's performance is the restaurant level margin resilience that the company has enjoyed.

Again, not to pick on other companies like Sweetgreen, but the recent consumer recession has had a huge impact on store level margins with margins falling from 20.2% in Q3 of 2024 to 13.1% in Q3 of 2025:

[2024] Restaurant level margin for the third quarter was 20.2%

[2025] Restaurant level margin was 13.1%

This is a considerable drop. With each Sweetgreen restaurant losing revenue and producing less cash flow, management at that company has less to pay corporate salaries and reinvest.

Relatively, CAVA has done incredibly well through the turmoil and the company's restaurant level profit margin came in at 24.6% in Q3 2025 - only one percent down from Q3 of 2024, when the company turned in a result of 25.6%:

Prior to the recent consumer recession, CAVA was experimenting with new menu items and operational enhancements to boost restaurant profit margins. Obviously, many of these have not borne fruit yet as a result of the consumer slowdown.

That said, as the consumer advances and the company continues executing as they’ve proven capable of doing, I expect profit margins to move closer to 30%.

CAVA's most recent result of 24.6% also came in ahead of mega competitor Chipotle, which reported restaurant level margins 10 basis points lower in Q3 of 2025:

[2025] Restaurant level margin was 24.5%.

Between the company's stable (and hopefully rebounding) same store sales growth and robust restaurant level margins, it means that for every new store the company opens, the stronger and stronger the company's bottom line earnings will be.

Reason 3: The Valuation

Finally, it’s worth pointing out that CAVA is trading at an incredibly discounted multiple right now.

When the stock IPO'd, it traded at a price to sales ratio of roughly ~7.1x, and currently the stock trades at a price to sales ratio of roughly ~7.3x.

In other words, right now, that you can buy shares in CAVA at roughly the same price as the company's first trading price:

Throughout the last two and a half years, the company has mostly traded at a much higher valuation - anywhere from 10x to 20x sales. Obviously these premium sales multiples were during a period where the consumer was strong and growth was robust, but if we see a rebound in the consumer, then there’s no reason we won’t get a higher average multiple, potentially a premium one, in the coming 12 to 24 months.

Additionally, on the bottom line, CAVA didn’t turn a profit until the start of 2024, and since then the stock's earnings multiple has slowly decreased from roughly 577x to only 60x today:

This ~90% drop in the earnings multiple is mostly due to the company's surging earnings, though some of it can also be attributed to the falling share price.

As a result, when compared to itself historically, CAVA looks attractively priced for growth.

When compared with fast growing, high margin fast casual peers like Wingstop (WING) and Chipotle, CAVA also appears reasonably priced. While the company's non-GAAP earnings multiples remain high, CAVA trades at a comparably priced PEG ratio: 4.0, vs Wingstop’s 4.5 and Chipotle’s 3.9:

If CAVA can produce the revenue growth analysts expect, then the company's P/S will also drop under 3x by the end of the decade, something I think appears highly achievable:

To be clear, I don't expect that CAVA's multiple will ever fall that low, but it shows just how much growth analysts are expecting as the existing store count expands.

I anticipate a 6x+ P/S ratio over the interim, and even potentially over the long term if restaurant-level margins remain intact. Even though CAVA doesn't return capital to shareholders at present, a stable multiple and the continued growth trajectory here have the potential to compound an investment.

Going forward, between CAVA's efficient operations, strong product market fit, financials, and relatively attractive price, I believe the stock looks like a Strong Buy.

Risks

Of course, there are risks associated with investing in CAVA. The core risk here, at least in my view, is if macro conditions around consumer sentiment don’t meaningfully improve in 2026.

To be clear, I expect that the company will produce positive returns even if the macro doesn’t improve materially - as a result of the aforementioned items. However, to get true 'Strong Buy'-like performance, the macro will need to cooperate.

This also brings up the question of opportunity cost. If the macro remains a headwind, then holding CAVA, as opposed to other stocks that are growing faster, doesn’t make as much sense.

Finally, in terms of true negative downside risks, CAVA's multiple at 60x earnings and 7x sales is still expensive versus the market, and in the event of a market correction, the company could see its shares sink much lower.

Additionally, if CAVA fails to deliver meaningful menu innovation or can’t boost restaurant level operating margins above the mid 20%'s due to competition from peers, then the overall upside of owning CAVA may be less interesting than expected, which could dent the stock.

To be clear, I’m expecting CAVA's value prop and strong execution to continue into the future, but competition is always a risk, especially in the restaurant business.

Summary

Overall, CAVA faces a number of risks including ongoing macro headwinds, competition from peers, and a nominally high valuation.

However, the company's strong execution through a difficult macro, robust unit economics, and strong growth prospects outweigh this downside.

Trading at a relative song, I see shares of CAVA as a Strong Buy.

Cheers.

data.title: The Future Of The Megabanks
data.description:
I believe that the megabanks are worth watching.

I believe that the megabanks are worth investing in.

I have recently written about the opportunity of investing in the megabanks.

Telis Demos writes in the Wall Street Journal about the fact that "All six of the U.S. megabanks--Bank of America, Citigroup, Goldman Sachs, JPMorgan Chase, Morgan Stanley, and Wells Fargo--beat the S&P 500 last year."

Demos cautions us about investing in these large entities.

"Certainly, there is risk to banks in an unpredictable political environment."

"But...", Mr. Demos goes on....

Here is the picture of how the megabanks performed in 2025.

Wow!!!

Mr. Demos seems to think that investing in the megabanks is not such a bad idea.

He comments: "On the whole, (the Trump administration's broader economic agenda) ought to keep banks firing on most, if not all, cylinders in 2026."

But I think that we need to consider more than just what the Trump economic policy agenda might do.

The megabanks, I believe, have been doing things for themselves, and it seems to be paying off.

And, Mr. Demos concedes to this when he comments about how the megabanks have kept performing over time...even as concern grew about how the economy was doing.

The environment over the past three years..." plenty of investors and economists have been predicting a recession or market downturn for three years now...."

But, as Demos points out, that "markets and the economy" continued to "power ahead."

How did this happen?

Well, I have been writing during this period of time about how the Federal Reserve has made sure that the commercial banking system had plenty of money...even, at times, excessive amounts of money.

And, I have been writing about how the megabanks performed during this period of time.

I particularly call attention to the performance of JPMorgan Chase (JPM).

Look at the latest results of JPMorgan Chase. These are from the press release from the fourth quarter performance of the bank and the full-year 2025 numbers.

Here is the connection: in the fourth quarter of 2025, JPMorganChase earned a return on tangible common equity of 18 percent.

For the full year of 2025, the bank earned a return on tangible common equity of 20 percent.

How good was this performance?

Well, in financial circles, if a commercial bank earns a return on tangible common equity of 20 percent...it is performing exceptionally well.

And, how well has JPMorgan performed over the past ten years?

Here are the numbers.

2018 17 %

2019 19 %

2020 14 %

2021 23 %

2022 18 %

2023 21 %

2024 22 %

2025 20%

These are exceptional numbers.

An ROTCE in excess of 20 percent?

AT the top of the list!!!!!

And, JPMorganChase has not achieved these numbers by squeezing its capital base.

The bank's standard CET1 capital ratio is 14.5 percent. Again, exceptional.

Jamie Dimon, the chairman and CEO of JPMorgan Chase, provides this reflection on the bank's performance.

"These results were the product of strong execution, years of investment, a favorable market backdrop, and selective deployment of excess capital."

Mr. Dimon is very proud of the bank's performance.

The plan...is to continue seeking a continuation of this exceptional performance.

Yet, the press played down the year's achievement. Note the headline in the New York Times: "JPMorgan Made Less Money Last Year, Facing Pressure on Multiple Fronts."

The article goes on..."It wasn't a calamity--the total for 2024 was a record $59 billion--but it was noteworthy...."

No, it wasn't a calamity....

A 20 percent ROTCE is just in the highest category of business performance.

And, it looks as if all the megabanks did well.

And, it looks as if the investment community agreed with this.

Back when I was running banks, back in the 1980s and 1990s, the big push was for getting these megabanks up into the 15 percent range...a range that really looked exceptional at the time.

Jamie Dimon...and JPMorgan Chase...set his sights high.

Analysts seemed to think that Mr. Dimon was a little "starry-eyed."

Getting to a 15 percent ROTCE seemed to be a "wishful" dream.

But, the megabanks got their act together...given the leadership of Mr. Dimon.

And, the Federal Reserve instituted a new type of monetary policy that the megabanks, I believe, took full advantage of.

The megabanks were able to restructure.

And, now we seem to be in an age where the stock market just seems to go up...and up...and up.

The stocks of the megabanks just seem to go up...and up...and up.

And, Mr. Demos concludes his article with the suggestion that "as long as investors continue to believe the economy writ large is going to be run hot, big-bank stocks are likely to keep chugging ahead."

data.title: Morgan Stanley Keeps Pushing
data.description:
Morgan Stanley (MS) is a bank stock that we have traded several times. Major bank reporting season is underway for Q4 2025, and as we know, 2025 was a great year for banking. Major global banks that reported yesterday disappointed a bit, but while Morgan Stanley is a bank, but is not exactly focused on traditional banking. It is more of an investment bank and wealth management firm, and it has delivered results that the Street likes.

We see a continued strong 2026 ahead, and the stock, while having moved dramatically, still has upside. With markets just off all-time highs, investment banking and trading income have led the bank to put out a solid Q4 earnings report. In this column, we check back in with our favorite investment bank.

Headline numbers strong

The bar is pretty high far banks but they are largely beating estimates. With the market starting Q4 weak, but finishing strong, we expected investment banking would continue to perform well. Traditional banking continues to enjoy strong margins overall, but the trading side of the investment banks do well in a volatile market which is what we the first half of Q4. Overall, a solid quarter indeed was registered.

Morgan Stanley reported net revenues of $17.9 billion in Q4. This was a solid outperformance. Revenues were actually up from last year a strong 10.4%. increase from last year's quarter. These revenues beat analysts' consensus by $140 million. The top line beat was a driver of strength, while expenses were well-managed. The bank has been working to be more efficient. Each segment put out a record performance and the margins were strong. Consolidated pre-tax margins were 32%, up from 30% a year-ago. The top-line rising with respectable margins led to a jump in net income to $4.4 billion, or $2.68 per share, compared with net income of $3.7 billion, or $2.22 per share, last year. This was a beat by $0.23. Solid performance indeed.

Morgan Stanley Q4 2025 earnings segment performance

As noted, Morgan Stanley is an investment-style bank, and trading activity is a huge driver of the bank's results. Let's discuss key segment highlights to be aware of. The "Institutional Securities" segment saw a jump in revenue from last year. The reported revenues here were $7.93 billion were up $0.7 billion from last year. There were higher advisory revenues as there has been more merger and acquisition activity this year. Underwriting was strong, as was equity. Fixed income however dipped $0.17 billion, one blemish.

In the "Investment Management" segment revenues were up nearly $100 million from a year ago. They came in at $1.72 billion, versus $1.64 billion a year ago. This was driven by higher asset management and related fees. There were also higher assets under management and, of course, the market had a lot of activity in Q4, and ended higher. Revenues in asst management comprise almost all of this segment, as revenues were $1.65 billion. Performance-based revenues were also down $17 million to $71 million. Total expenses were up just $23 million, so there was a strong margin here.

Over in the "Wealth Management" side of the business, there was a 13% increase in revenue. The segment reported net revenues for the current quarter of $8.42 billion, compared with $7.48 billion a year ago. Asset management revenue was up $614 million, reflecting higher asset levels and fees-based cash flows. This is very strong. Transactional revenue also increased 17% to $1.14 billion. Further, net interest income improved as margins were stronger than last year. This has to do with changes in the deposit mix, the rates on deposits, and the rates on lending.

Morgan Stanley Q4 2025 earnings efficiency ratio

The efficiency ratio is a metric we follow for all banks, large or small. The most efficient banks see ratios under 50%. Typically larger banks are slightly less efficient than smaller regional banks in our experience. Morgan Stanley's efficiency ratio, in part because of their operations as more of an investment bank, has consistently had efficiency ratios above 70% since we have been in the name. That said, the bank broke under this 70% this year. For the entire year it was a 68% efficiency, versus 71% last year. And for Q4, it was also 68%. This is strong, and better than the historical pace in recent years.

Morgan Stanley Dividend

The bank has continued to raise its dividend. Now the dividend is up to $1.00 per share each quarter. With the huge move in shares over the last year the yield is just 2.21%, a far cry from the over 4% when were pounding the table to buy shares. That said, we continue to think this bank stock should be considered by dividend growth investors for long-term investment on any future weakness. We also should point out the bank repurchased $1.5billion of its outstanding common stock during the quarter as part of its repurchase program, and bought back $4.6 billion during the year. These repurchases further boost shareholder value and improve EPS potential.

Final thoughts

Morgan Stanley's Q4 earnings were better than expected on the top and bottom line. The market activity between only some periods of volatility but new highs being made has helped the bank's segments. Further merger activity and underwriting is elevated compared to last year and prior years. While rates are being cut, we see margin power remaining strong as yields on loans are still high, while the costs of deposits come down. Wealth management activity remains strong. The bank is also more efficient. We think there is more in the tank here for shares to push higher, even if MS stock has moved substantially the last year.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: BE Semiconductor: This Chip Packaging Leader Could Win From AI Memory Shortage
data.description:
In my prior report, I marked BE Semiconductor Industries N.V. (BESIY) (BESVF), or Besi, as a Buy driven by red-hot demand for data center expansion that could result in higher demand for die-attach and packaging equipment. Since my last report, the stock has reached my price target, the company provided a trading update, and we are seeing industry dynamics that could be positive for Besi. In this report, I will discuss the dynamics and trading update and review my price target, explaining why a premium valuation is justified.

Besi Could Address Package Bottleneck For AI Memory

In recent weeks and months, we have seen consumer memory prices going through the roof. The reason is that there is not enough capacity to fulfill demand for High Bandwidth Memory, which is memory typically used for AI applications. As a result, manufacturers are dialing back production on consumer-grade memory modules to assign the capacity to the manufacturing of advanced memory modules. Even then, we see that there is not enough capacity to fulfill demand, and that has sent not only consumer grade memory prices up, but also led to assumptions that HBM pricing strength will persist and increase.

The main bottleneck is packaging CoWoS, or Chip-on-Wafer-on-Substrate, which is a 2.5D/3D packaging technology used to integrate chips like HBM on a package substrate. Besi does not manufacture chips, but it happens to be one of the leaders in advanced packaging. The revenue in this market is set to grow by almost 19% annually through 2028.

Specific to HBM, the company has sales opportunities for TCB or Thermo-Compression Bonding tools and Hybrid Bonding tools. We do note that lead times for these advanced tools can be 9-12 months. So, it is not the case that a company orders from Besi today to address the packaging capacity shortage, and the tooling is shipped tomorrow. That is also why DRAM and particularly HBM pricing are expected to remain robust this year. It is for the simple fact that the growth of packaging capacity falls short of demand, and adding additional capacity means ordering a machine today and having it ready for service in late 2026 or early 2027.

Beyond that, the space needs to be there to put the machine to work. So, adding capacity is not going to happen overnight, but the more pressing the bottleneck becomes, the bigger the need to make investments to add capacity.

Besi Reports Big Order Jump In Q4 2025

Besi provided a trading update, and that update confirmed that the company is seeing an uptick in order activity, likely to address capacity shortages. Q4 2025 orders increased 105% year-on-year to €250 million and 43% sequentially. The company noted that orders included hybrid bonding tools. Furthermore, the company said that its Q4 results are expected to be at the favorable end of the guidance.

Going Aggressive On BE Semiconductor Stock Valuation

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and update the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

For BE Semiconductor, I am changing the valuation multiple to better align with the strength seen over the past years, as I believe the company will see an acceleration in sales growth starting this year and stretching beyond the forecast period as capacity additions are required to support demand in 2027 and beyond. Those capacity additions to mitigate bottlenecks should drive sales for TCB and Hybrid Bonding equipment. We note that changes to EBITDA estimates have been modest at less than a percent.

Free cash flow estimates have been improved somewhat more, with a 5% upward revision for 2025, roughly the same for 2026, and up 10% for 2027 for a total upward revision of around 6%. I continue to note that BE Semiconductor stock is not cheap at all, but applying the EV/EBITDA multiple that we have seen over the past three years, we still see a 9% upside this year, and the upside in 2027 is even more appealing.

Starting this year, the expectation is that margins will increase drastically, which likely is driven by improved pricing and volumes of shipments translating to strong cash flow growth. The company is also expected to have a cash position allowing it to return value to shareholders while remaining near a net-cash position.

Conclusion: BE Semiconductor Commands A Premium

BE Semiconductor stock is definitely not cheap. However, what we are currently seeing is that memory pricing is increasing as demand for high-bandwidth memory outpaces capacity additions. As a result, memory sales are locked in for 2026, and tightness could last into 2028, and that means that in the years ahead, to ease the CoWoS packaging constraints, additional equipment needs to be purchased, from which I expect BE Semiconductor to benefit substantially as one of the leaders in semiconductor assembly equipment.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: ClearBridge International Growth EAFE Strategy Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
By Elisa Mazen, Michael Feldman, CFA, Michael Testorf, CFA and Pawel Wroblewski, CFA

Preparing for Improving Growth Earnings

Market Overview

International equities delivered solid gains in the fourth quarter to cap one of the best years for overseas stocks in decades. The core benchmark MSCI EAFE Index rose 4.9% in the quarter to finish up 31.2% for 2025, outperforming the S&P 500 Index’s gains of 2.7% for the quarter and 17.9% for the year. The market rally was broad with the MSCI Emerging Markets Index advancing 4.7% for the quarter and 33.6% for the year.

Outside the U.S., growth stocks delivered strong absolute returns but meaningfully underperformed value stocks for both the fourth quarter and full year. The MSCI EAFE Growth Index rose 1.9% for the quarter, trailing the MSCI EAFE Value Index by 590 basis points, and 20.8% for the year, trailing its value counterpart by over 2,100 bps, the widest dispersion among investment styles in the 50-year history of the indexes.

Exhibit 1: International Value Growth Dispersion at All-Time Highs

In such a value-dominated period, the ClearBridge International Growth Strategy underperformed its primary MSCI EAFE benchmark for the fourth quarter and full year. The Strategy slightly underperformed its secondary MSCI EAFE Growth benchmark for the quarter but outperformed for the year. Annual results were supported by strong stock picking in Asia Ex Japan, Japan, Europe and Canada, offsetting weakness in the U.K. and emerging markets.

Performance Highlights

Value has worked internationally because inexpensive stocks are the most direct beneficiaries of the enormous stimulus measures being approved in Germany, other parts of Europe and Japan. Growth stocks, in contrast, are a much smaller part of core benchmarks. In Europe, which represents roughly three-quarters of developed market indexes, the information technology (IT) sector is quite small — at 8.5% it is the fifth-largest sector in the EAFE — and concentrated in cyclical areas.

Exhibit 2: MSCI Growth vs. Value Quarterly Performance

As a growth manager, we are selective in owning the companies that are typically thought of as value in our structural growth segment, which is only one part of our three growth areas. This structural growth bucket includes more cyclical stocks and turnaround stories, enabling us to participate in most types of market environments. However, with the 21 percentage point differential of value over growth in 2025, growth strategies will struggle to outperform a core benchmark with their value component. We have increased our structural growth exposure throughout the year, predominantly by increasing allocations to European and U.K. banks, where we are seeing a step change in earnings. U.K.-based NatWest Group (NWG) was a leading contributor during the quarter, and we think banks can continue to work as we believe that interest rates will be stable and loan growth will likely increase along with higher fiscal stimulus and to fund AI and energy transitions. Those new loans will be more profitable with stable credit quality. Bank valuations remain inexpensive with excess capital and strong cash generation supporting high dividends.

The Strategy saw strength in holdings supporting the buildout of AI workloads during the quarter. These were led by Germany’s Siemens Energy (SMEGF), a supplier of electrical equipment to power data centers, semiconductor equipment maker Tokyo Electron (TOELY), Canada’s Celestica, a provider of technology solutions for cloud platforms, and Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM), the world’s largest contract manufacturer of high-end chips.

Relative performance in the fourth quarter was negatively impacted by weakness in the consumer discretionary sector. Sea Limited (SE), the Singapore-based e-commerce, gaming and fintech provider, was hurt by profit taking over potential increasing competitive concerns after the shares rose 80% in the first eight months of the year. Japan’s Sony (SONY), whose diversified media, entertainment and technology businesses are headlined by its PlayStation franchise, traded lower after the spinout of its Sony Financial Group. Additionally, the sharp rise in DRAM prices created uncertainty around margin pressure in the company’s gaming division, further weighing on the share price. Positively, management confirmed that it had stockpiled DRAM ahead of price increases, retaining pricing power to offset any future potential margin pressure.

Media and technology conglomerate Tencent (TCEHY) was caught up in general weakness in the Chinese market while Zai Lab (ZLAB) was down over the push out of its move to breakeven profitability into 2027.

The health care sector has overall underperformed for the year, although selective stocks within the portfolio did quite well as U.S. policy and most favored nation pricing discussions were taking place well into the end of the year. Most of these issues are moderating today as corporations have adjusted selective pricing of products and capital spending to U.S. demands. European biopharmaceutical makers Argenx (ARGX) and UCB (UCBJF) as well as the U.K.’s AstraZeneca (AZN), meanwhile, were bright spots in the pharmaceutical side of health care. The medtech side of the sector has remained fairly weak, as large companies with broad portfolios geographically have faced worries about their U.S. growth rates as well as emerging Chinese competition.

While health care stocks have been weak, we view many of them as a compelling innovation source, notably in biotechnology. Some of these companies do not yet have commercial revenue but are developing new products (for example, China has been aggressively developing and out-licensing early stage antibody drug conjugates products that target cancer cells that could be the next clinical breakthrough large cap pharmaceuticals companies are seeking).

Portfolio Positioning

The health care sector was a focus of activity in the quarter with the repurchases of EssilorLuxottica (ESLOF) and Galderma Group (GALDY) and the addition of Roche (RHHBY). We see opportunity for EssilorLuxottica, an Italian-French maker of sunglasses and corrective lenses, and expect its digital glasses partnership with Meta Platforms (META) to be bigger than the market is discounting. The company’s first FDA-approved myopia lenses have only just begun to roll out while high-margin hearing aid glasses provide a lot of optionality. Swiss dermatology products provider Galderma is expected to grow revenue in injectables, a market benefiting from secular tailwinds from an aging population and new product launches. Its Nemluvio drug aimed at atopic dermatitis and prurigo nodularis has exceeded expectations.

Swiss biopharmaceutical and diagnostics company Roche reported positive Phase III data in the fourth quarter for giredestrant in breast cancer and fenebrutinib in multiple sclerosis, paving the way for new treatments with peak sales opportunities of $5 billion each. These two products should enable Roche to grow revenues through the loss of exclusivity period for several cancer and MS drugs and secure its place among the pharmaceutical companies that can grow revenue for the next 10+ years.

Roche replaces Danish GLP-1 pharmaceutical maker Novo Nordisk, a long-term holding that has faced numerous challenges internally and externally. The rise of obesity medications has undermined its once leading position in the market, complicated by the rise of compounders, specifically in the U.S. This has led to steep price decreases for treatment. We sold the position as we do not believe the changes it has made to date will close the competitive gap relative to Eli Lilly.

In aggregate, we initiated five positions in the quarter and exited 10 others. European discount airline Ryanair (RYAAY) was another repurchase after many years. The company is benefiting from several catalysts that could drive positive earnings surprises, including an improving industry structure that should allow for: expansion and increasing market share; operational efficiency improvements; capacity shortages and favorable fuel prices; as well as management confidence in passenger growth and profitability.

We also added Heidelberg Materials (HDLMY), a German supplier of building materials for residential, commercial and infrastructure construction. The company could see upside to margins from improving capacity utilization in Europe and its advantages in implementing decarbonization technologies in the production of cement. European construction activity is at 30% lower levels than pre-COVID, paving the way for a recovery that could be triggered by vigorous German stimulus that should be followed across the EU.

Trane Technologies (TT) was among the portfolio’s larger sales. Our investment thesis changed due to weakness in the overall HVAC market rather than Trane specifically, with residential and “light commercial” HVAC sales declining much deeper than expected, making it difficult to reach our sales growth expectations for the high-multiple stock. Within industrials, we remain focused on Siemens Energy and Schneider Electric, where we believe data centers will remain supportive for earnings revisions.

We also exited Italian bank UniCredit (UNCRY) as it reached its price target, as well as U.K.-based industrial gases producer Linde (LIN) which we viewed as having limited upside. A small position in Nemetschek (NEMTF), a German software maker serving construction, engineering and design customers, was sold to get ahead of the AI disruptions faced by application software companies.

Outlook

Better earnings can drive growth stocks higher, and we see challenges to future earnings for growth companies falling into three categories: tariffs, technology and transition. These issues have created volatility around earnings and impacted more highly rated growth stocks. Many of these are in the secular growth bucket and should see a reassertion of growth as the advantages from AI begin to come through and new ways of monetizing their businesses emerge to drive growth rates higher. Lower interest rates will certainly help stocks, but we think that to reassert market leadership — specifically in technology software and hardware — it will come down to improved earnings.

Investors have shunned China since COVID, so much so that indexes were being created ex-China. However, while China is a complicated market with many local players and several exchanges, the innovation there is not only rapid, but it also happens at much lower prices. The next five years of Chinese industrial policy will see companies consolidate and dominate at the higher end of the value chain across industries like pharmaceuticals, battery materials/storage, solar energy and technology. With lower valuations and a move to higher profitability overall, this is a market where we need to have exposure.

Exhibit 3: China Valuations Compelling

Increased non-U.S. defense spending is here to stay as the role of NATO evolves from a regime of U.S. leadership to more spending and participation from European members . We have seen a rebuilding of inventories in the European Union and efforts to maintain a steady supply of defense infrastructure. Japan has also increased its defense spending and its new prime minister has a priority in ensuring there is a strong military to counter new and existing threats on its own.

Despite a relative performance comeback for international equities in 2025, valuations remain historically attractive compared to their U.S. peers. Overseas markets continue to offer growth franchises with best-in-class business models and innovation comparable to the U.S. but at more reasonable multiples. International companies are clear leaders across industries such as semiconductor equipment, biopharmaceuticals, luxury goods and advanced manufacturing and are well-positioned amid the current secular trends of AI and broader defense spending.

Portfolio Highlights

During the fourth quarter, the ClearBridge International Growth EAFE Strategy underperformed its MSCI EAFE Index benchmark and MSCI EAFE Growth Index secondary benchmark. On an absolute basis, the Strategy produced positive contributions across five of the nine sectors in which it was invested (out of 11 total), with the financials and IT sectors the top contributors while the consumer discretionary sector was the chief detractor.

We have been monitoring on a regular basis and reporting to our clients on the performance of the MSCI EAFE (core), MSCI EAFE Value and MSCI EAFE Growth Indexes in order to provide context on market movements for growth investors.

Relative to the MSCI EAFE Index, overall stock selection and sector allocation detracted from performance. In particular, stock selection in consumer discretionary, health care, industrials, materials and financials and an underweight to financials weighed on results. On the positive side, stock selection in IT and an underweight to communication services contributed to performance.

Relative to the MSCI EAFE Growth Index, overall stock selection detracted but was partially offset by positive sector allocation effects. In particular, stock selection in consumer discretionary, health care and industrials weighed on results. On the positive side, stock selection in financials, IT, communication services and consumer staples, an underweight to communication services and an overweight to financials contributed to performance.

On an individual stock basis, the largest contributors to relative returns in the quarter were Siemens Energy in industrials, NatWest Group in financials, Argenx in health care, Tokyo Electron in IT and Inditex in consumer discretionary. The greatest detractors from relative returns included positions in Sea Limited in consumer discretionary, Sony in consumer discretionary, Tencent in communication services, Zai Lab in health care and RELX (RELX) in industrials.

In addition to the transactions mentioned above, we closed positions in Constellation Software (CNSWF) and Lenovo (LNVGY) in IT, Marks and Spencer Group (MAKSY) in consumer discretionary as well as Tokio Marine (TKOMY) and Sony Financial Group (SFGYY) in financials.

Elisa Mazen, Head of Global Growth, Portfolio Manager

Michael Feldman, CFA, Michael Feldman, CFA

Michael Testorf, CFA, Portfolio Manager

Pawel Wroblewski, CFA

data.title: Boston Scientific: Reclaiming Neurovascular Dominance Through Thrombectomy Precision
data.description:
Introduction

Boston Scientific Corporation (BSX) is in the news this morning because it entered into a definitive agreement to acquire Penumbra (PEN) for around $14.5B in a cash and stock transaction, which is expected to be closed this year, subject to PEN’s shareholder approval and any other “customary closing conditions.”

This move looks to expand the company’s overall reach in many new key verticals that have shown a lot of promising growth, especially within the fast-growing segments in vascular science, and complement its existing portfolio to combat the global cardiovascular rise.

Penumbra is a leading provider of innovative mechanical thrombectomy products that help remove blood clots that cause blockages, as well as expertise in neurovascular offerings that help combat stroke.

Penumbra Synergies

PEN’s thrombectomy products will complement BSX’s already robust Cardiovascular and MedSurg divisions, which will enable cross-selling and technological integration when it comes to minimally invasive vascular therapies. This is a move to gain market leadership in mechanical thrombectomy and to expand its vascular intervention portfolio. We can see that BSX highlighted the most promising products from Penumbra in the latest M&A slideshow. These products include Ruby Coil and POD coil, which are used to block peripheral blood vessels in case of aneurysms, bleeding, or tumors. This part of the peripheral segment is targeting around high-single-digit growth. Additionally, in the peripheral segment, the company expects PEN’s Lightning Flash and Bolt systems to lead mid-teens growth as well.

Within the Neurovascular segment, BSX lacks a dedicated thrombectomy like the Penumbra System, which, when the deal closes, will help fill the gap and allow BSX to compete in this field. Stryker (SYK) is a company that offers neurovascular thrombectomy and is one of the bigger players in the field, which is kind of funny considering they got this from BSX over a decade ago. Back then, the company was focusing on something else completely, so it made sense to divest it at the time; however, it seems like the management sees a lot of potential in the field going forward, hence why they are reentering it.

So, I can see this segment being primarily additive, as BSX didn’t have much exposure for over a decade in this field. This will beef up BSX’s offerings in the respective segments significantly, as the company sees PEN’s pipeline growing at an 11% WAMGR, weighted average monthly growth rate.

Penumbra’s Performance

Let’s look at what Penumbra looks to achieve for the fiscal year 2025. For Q4, the company expects to see around $384m in sales, which is over 21% growth y/y. For the full year, revenues will be around $1.4B, around 17.4% y/y growth. There seemed to be some headwinds from China, where revenues declined due to some macroeconomic pressures and delayed hospital purchases, so without the China exposure, growth was around 24.5%. The U.S. thrombectomy surged over 20%.

The company also has a decent balance sheet, with over $321m in cash and $149m in marketable securities, and no long-term debt, besides some operating lease liabilities of around $180m.

Margins across the board have been improving significantly as its products have become a lot more prominent in the field. This, over time, will be margin accretive for BSX.

Effect On BSX Performance

So, the company expects this deal to be dilutive in the first year, meaning the transaction will dilute around $0.06 to $0.08 in terms of adjusted earnings. After the first year, the management expects to either see the acquisition have no more negative effects or to be more accretive, and in the year after that, the acquisition should be accretive to the business in general. The same impacts are expected to affect the company’s GAAP EPS.

These effects are due to the way the company is looking to finance this acquisition. The deal is for $14.5B, financed via cash on hand and new debt. The combination of the two will equal $11B, while the rest will be financed via share issuance, around $3.5B in shares. The company expects to fund the deal with around 27% in shares of BSX. So, seeing that the company has around $1.2B in cash, it is safe to assume most of the cash will come from new debt, which will increase BSX’s debt position to over $20B. This will increase its interest expenses significantly. I’m assuming interest will essentially double from what it is right now. In the latest quarter, the company paid $87m in interest expense, so that will most likely go to $180m+ going forward, which is still not terrible.

The company’s operating profit for the latest quarter was around $1B, so the interest coverage ratio will still be over 5x. However, since it still increases, this will affect the company’s bottom line and the extra issuance of shares as well, hence the expected dilutive effect in the first year. Additionally, Penumbra will add quite a bit to operating income going forward, which will help offset a lot of the dilution effects.

Regulatory Pushback?

I believe the risk of regulatory pushback is minimal. It would be a different story if BSX hadn’t sold its thrombectomy business to Stryker over a decade ago, but BSX has very limited, or essentially no, exposure in this segment currently, which means the regulators will not be very harsh on the deal. Stryker and Medtronic (MDT) are dominating this market, so another significant player entering the market via an acquisition is a good thing overall.

Investor Takeaway

I remain bullish on the company’s future. The management is looking to expand its reach to different segments that seem to be carrying an ever-increasing margin profile from what we saw in PEN’s performance. Sure, the dilution isn’t ideal, but I view it as a short-term pain for a long-term gain type of situation.

Re-entering high-growth neurovascular and peripheral segments via already-proven thrombectomy leaders like Penumbra, BSX is going to be a serious contender to take a decent chunk of the market share from the current dominant players mentioned earlier.

The financial position of BSX remains decent. The additional debt is going to have some negatives, but it is still very much manageable.
